h1: Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO2) Calculator by Altitude
description: Calculate normal SpO2 blood oxygen saturation levels by altitude. Reference ranges for sea level to high elevation.
intro: Normal blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) values come from large-scale population studies (NHANES, WHO). These reference ranges give you a framework to interpret your results, but your personal health context matters most. This calculator shows what's normal SpO2 at different altitudes. All values are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **SpO2 altitude reference table**. Direct application with your input values. Normal oxygen saturation varies by elevation.

fields.label: Altitude (m)
fields.help: Elevation in meters above sea level.

explanation:
**Normal health values** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. These are guidelines—your personal medical history always takes priority.

## How It's Calculated

Blood oxygen saturation at different altitudes.

This calculator applies the reference formula with your entered values, checking against typical ranges and alerting you if values fall outside normal parameters.

## Normal Adult Reference Ranges

| Parameter | Normal Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO2 (Oxygen Saturation) | 95-100% |
| Fasting Blood Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (Men) / >50 (Women) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult a qualified specialist. Values are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What is a normal SpO2 level?
faq.a: 95-100% at sea level is considered normal for healthy adults.
Yes. At 2,500-3,500m elevation, 90-94% SpO2 can be normal due to acclimatization.
This indicates significant hypoxia (low blood oxygen). Seek medical attention.
Generally reliable, but not perfect. Extreme readings should prompt a doctor's visit.

faq.q: When should I worry about low SpO2?
faq.a: If SpO2 stays below 90%, or if you experience shortness of breath or bluish skin color.

faq.q: Should I check SpO2 after COVID-19?
faq.a: Yes—test with light activity. A significant drop compared to baseline warrants evaluation.

faq.q: What's the maximum altitude without supplemental oxygen?
faq.a: Depends on acclimatization, but generally up to 5,500m for most people without extra oxygen.

faq.q: When should I see a doctor about SpO2?
faq.a: If readings are consistently outside normal ranges, if you have symptoms, or if there's family history of heart or metabolic disease.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula—no external data updates required
